stage.name: CV-X-IF: custom extension without breaking the core story
stage.description: A deep-dive on CV-X-IF issue/commit/result contracts and safe coprocessor design boundaries.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: CV-X-IF is an extension contract, not a magic side door

content.title: CV-X-IF is an extension contract, not a magic side door
content.subtitle: CVA6 can offload accepted custom work, but the offload still has issue, commit, result, and limitation rules.
content.thesis: Teach CV-X-IF as another functional-unit contract that must respect CVA6 ordering and state discipline.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: CV-X-IF Interface and Coprocessor
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CV-X-IF extends CVA6 with external coprocessors; CVA6 implements issue, commit, and result interfaces while compressed and memory interfaces are not implemented yet.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson is CV-X-IF: custom extension without breaking the core story. CVA6 can offload accepted custom work, but the offload still has issue, commit, result, and limitation rules. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

actions.text: This lesson is CV-X-IF: custom extension without breaking the core story. CVA6 can offload accepted custom work, but the offload still has issue, commit, result, and limitation rules. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

scenes.title: Three implemented interfaces define the core contract

content.title: Issue, commit, and result are the supported core path
content.subtitle: The CVA6 chapter is explicit about which mandatory interfaces are implemented.

cards.title: Issue
cards.body: CVA6 sends operands, instruction, valid, and scoreboard-related identity.

cards.title: Commit
cards.body: The commit side communicates whether accepted offloaded work is committed.

cards.title: Result
cards.body: The coprocessor returns results and exceptions through the writeback/result path.

cards.title: Not yet
cards.body: Compressed, memory, and memory-result interfaces are not implemented yet.

content.takeaway: The supported subset matters as much as the interface name.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: CV-X-IF Interface and Coprocessor
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CV-X-IF extends CVA6 with external coprocessors; CVA6 implements issue, commit, and result interfaces while compressed and memory interfaces are not implemented yet.

content.speakerNotes: CV-X-IF is powerful, but the most important teaching point is the boundary. The CVA6 manual says the implemented interfaces are issue, commit, and result. It also says compressed and memory-related interfaces are not yet implemented. That means a custom coprocessor lesson must teach both what is possible and what must not be claimed.

actions.text: CV-X-IF is powerful, but the most important teaching point is the boundary. The CVA6 manual says the implemented interfaces are issue, commit, and result. It also says compressed and memory-related interfaces are not yet implemented. That means a custom coprocessor lesson must teach both what is possible and what must not be claimed.

scenes.title: Scoreboard ID links offload to backend identity

content.title: Offloaded work still needs identity
content.body: The CV-X-IF issue request links the scoreboard transaction ID to the coprocessor-side ID, so returned work can be tracked like other functional-unit results.
content.caption: Use the scoreboard model from Unit 05 when explaining CV-X-IF result identity.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: CV-X-IF Interface and Coprocessor
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CV-X-IF extends CVA6 with external coprocessors; CVA6 implements issue, commit, and result interfaces while compressed and memory interfaces are not implemented yet.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: Scoreboard submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The scoreboard tracks issued instructions, stores returned results by transaction ID, and exposes the oldest completed entry to commit.

content.speakerNotes: This is where earlier units pay off. The CV-X-IF chapter says the scoreboard transaction ID is connected to the issue request ID. That means offloaded work is not floating outside the backend. It has identity. It can return a result. It can participate in the same broad discipline as other functional-unit results.

actions.text: This is where earlier units pay off. The CV-X-IF chapter says the scoreboard transaction ID is connected to the issue request ID. That means offloaded work is not floating outside the backend. It has identity. It can return a result. It can participate in the same broad discipline as other functional-unit results.

content.title: A good CV-X-IF explanation includes the no-go zones

items.title: No CV-X-IF memory interface yet
items.body: Memory operations through the extension interface are not implemented in CVA6 yet.

items.title: No compressed interface yet
items.body: Custom compressed instruction handling is not part of the implemented subset.

items.title: Be careful with stateful coprocessors
items.body: Speculation and kill limitations make stateful coprocessors risky.

items.title: Use configuration gate
items.body: CV-X-IF can be enabled or disabled by configuration.

content.takeaway: Limitations are not weakness; they are part of the engineering contract.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: CV-X-IF Interface and Coprocessor
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CV-X-IF extends CVA6 with external coprocessors; CVA6 implements issue, commit, and result interfaces while compressed and memory interfaces are not implemented yet.

content.speakerNotes: Professional teaching includes limitations. CVA6 does not implement the CV-X-IF memory interface yet, and it does not implement the compressed interface yet. The recommendations also warn about stateful coprocessors because speculation information is kept in CVA6. Those limits are exactly what a designer needs before building an extension.

actions.text: Professional teaching includes limitations. CVA6 does not implement the CV-X-IF memory interface yet, and it does not implement the compressed interface yet. The recommendations also warn about stateful coprocessors because speculation information is kept in CVA6. Those limits are exactly what a designer needs before building an extension.

scenes.title: Check understanding: CV-X-IF: custom extension without breaking the core story

questions.question: Which CV-X-IF interfaces are implemented in CVA6 according to the manual?
questions.analysis: The memory and memory-result interfaces are not implemented yet in CVA6.

questions.question: Why does scoreboard ID matter for CV-X-IF?

options.label: It links offloaded work to backend instruction identity
It chooses the next PC directly

questions.analysis: The issue request ID is tied to scoreboard identity so results can be tracked.

questions.question: What should you avoid claiming about CV-X-IF in CVA6?

options.label: That CV-X-IF memory interface is already implemented
That custom register-to-register instructions are possible

questions.analysis: The manual explicitly says memory-related CV-X-IF interfaces are not implemented yet.

questions.question: What is the safe mental model?

options.label: A coprocessor functional-unit contract with explicit limitations
A replacement for the whole SoC

questions.analysis: CV-X-IF extends execution through a contract, not through magic system ownership.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: CV-X-IF Interface and Coprocessor
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CV-X-IF extends CVA6 with external coprocessors; CVA6 implements issue, commit, and result interfaces while compressed and memory interfaces are not implemented yet.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: interface requirements
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document names MEM, DBG, IRQ, XIF, and TRI requirements, including AXI memory interface, CLINT interrupt interface, CV-X-IF, and P-Mesh TRI.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: EX_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: EX_STAGE groups functional units such as ALU, Branch Unit, CSR buffer, Mult, load/store, and CVXIF.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: Scoreboard submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The scoreboard tracks issued instructions, stores returned results by transaction ID, and exposes the oldest completed entry to commit.

content.speakerNotes: This quiz checks the core judgment of CV-X-IF: custom extension without breaking the core story. The right answers should feel less like trivia and more like disciplined classification: what is the boundary, what is the source, what is the limitation, and what would we check next?

scenes.title: Summary: CV-X-IF: custom extension without breaking the core story

content.title: CV-X-IF: custom extension without breaking the core story
takeaways: Explain CV-X-IF support, limitations, and scoreboard identity without implying unsupported memory or compressed interfaces.
Turn every public explanation into an evidence-backed learning artifact.
content.nextStep: Next: P-Mesh and multi-core interface requirements, where the documented requirement is not the same as current configuration support.
content.closing: This is how OpenHW Explorer can teach serious open hardware without drifting into unsupported certainty.

sourceAnchors.claimSupported: EX_STAGE groups functional units such as ALU, Branch Unit, CSR buffer, Mult, load/store, and CVXIF.

content.speakerNotes: To summarize: Explain CV-X-IF support, limitations, and scoreboard identity without implying unsupported memory or compressed interfaces. Keep the claim small, keep the source visible, and keep the boundary honest. Next: P-Mesh and multi-core interface requirements, where the documented requirement is not the same as current configuration support.
